serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of spaghetti arrabbiata is approximately 300g (one plate), providing around 153 kcal, 4.2g protein, 4.4g fat, and 24.2g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti Arrabbiata — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spaghetti Arrabbiata (cr_id G00010588). All per_100g values preserved from the HPB canonical record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spaghetti arrabbiata as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked spaghetti (white, boiled, GI ~45–49) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor. The tomato-based sauce and olive oil slow gastric emptying, supporting a low-GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (24.2g per 300g serving) / 100 = 10.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 419mg/100g — borderline high; potassium 319mg/100g partially offsets but fibre at 1.8g/100g is below the 3g/100g rescue threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200g per serving and request reduced-salt sauce preparation, as a 300g serving delivers approximately 1,257mg sodium — over half the 2,000mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.05g/100g with estimated GI ~45 (low); sugar 5.5g/100g; dietary fibre 1.8g/100g; a 300g serving delivers approximately 24.2g total carbohydrate with glycemic load ~10.9
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spaghetti arrabbiata has a low estimated GI (~45) and moderate glycemic load (~10.9 per 300g serving), making it acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled; substituting wholemeal or legume-based pasta would further reduce glycemic impact and increase fibre intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 419mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 0.3g/100g; cholesterol is low at 2mg/100g; olive oil provides cardioprotective MUFA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat (0.3g/100g) and cholesterol (2mg/100g) profile is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the elevated sodium (419mg/100g) requires portion control and reduced-salt preparation to remain within cardiovascular-safe sodium targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli); olive oil provides anti-inflammatory MUFA; sodium 419mg/100g may contribute to joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Olive oil and garlic contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols beneficial for RA; however, tomato and chilli are nightshade vegetables that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals — patients should monitor personal response and request reduced-salt preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Arrabbiata is a classic Italian pasta dish consisting of spaghetti tossed in a spicy tomato-based sauce. The name 'arrabbiata' means 'angry' in Italian, referring to the heat from chilli. This Singapore restaurant version features homemade tomato sauce, XO chilli crunch, fresh basil, and parmigiano reggiano cheese. The dish is prepared by boiling spaghetti to al dente, then sautéing garlic and chilli in olive oil before adding tomato sauce and finishing with basil and grated parmesan. The result is a tangy, spicy, and savoury pasta with a firm texture and rich umami depth from the XO chilli crunch and aged cheese. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 51 kcal, 1.41g protein, 1.48g fat (of which only 0.3g is saturated), and 8.05g carbohydrates with 1.8g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 419mg/100g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 45 (low), with a glycemic load of ~10.9 per standard 300g serving. Olive oil contributes cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and anti-inflammatory polyphenols. Tomato and chilli are nightshade vegetables relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients. Suitable for vegetarians; contains gluten (wheat pasta) and dairy (parmigiano). Commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Italian and Western restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Spaghetti Arrabbiata (cr_id G00010588) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked white spaghetti as the closest analogous food, which is a well-established and defensible analogy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite pasta dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB source and consistent with olive-oil-based preparation. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond the HPB-reported fields are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this dish as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 0.3g/100g is estimated from the known fat composition of olive oil and parmigiano reggiano in a typical arrabbiata preparation, consistent with the HPB total fat of 1.48g/100g. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white spaghetti from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for spaghetti arrabbiata as a composite dish was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (45) × carbohydrate per 300g serving (24.15g) / 100 = 10.9. This dish is served in Singapore restaurants and is classified under Western/Italian cuisine; multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for Singapore-served dishes.
